sentences.text: I was performing magic-- magician-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] over there in the camp.
And I organized some singers.
And some people was capable to play accordion.
And some people was capable of acrobatic tricks, to do it.
And poetry, whatever, any skills you could do-- pick up.
And some people was talented, capable young people, older people-- capable.
And, even, there was performers, drama performers.
And there was one opera actor, too, with us.
And I have enough Jews to create something, to do it.
And we have very nice, good performance.
And then they sent us already, the group, to perform around the other camps.
Why, I would say around 350,000, 400,000 people were there--
--around Kolmya-- around Kolmya over there.
And it was lots of camps.
And we used to go in the other camps, which-- no pay.
And we used to perform over there, too.
Oh yes, yes.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A matter of fact, even-- they let you free over there to to go, there's nowhere to go.
It's--
--just to die-- the tundra over there and tigers and-- just to die.
Absolutely no point even to try.
I think it was in 1954.
I was--
I was-- altogether, it was eight years.
And five years without right to live.
I mean, I can't-- after prison, 25 years, I don't supposed to live 105 kilometers away from the big city.
And in 10 years, no right to vote.
The prison came to an end after eight-year term.
And I was a librarian.
And my obligation was to talk and to re-educated-- to re-educated the people, to tell them, to prove to them they was guilty and they have to correct themselves.
It was this kind-- type of work, like social work, to do it.
And it was editor for-- in our local, small, little paper.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one time, it came-- from from Moscow, they came, the big shots over there, to check out and have a big meeting.
And they have a big job to do it.
And I came out with a speech over there.
And I told them we're a little bit guilty for the government.
We have to say like this.
And we're trying the best to correct our mistake, what we did it.
In the future, not to call us enemies, to call us friends-- and we're going to prove it, and et cetera.
It was a speech.
And they was interesting to talk to me after.
They called me up.
And I told them, usually, you're putting in jail people to correct themselves.
He said yes.
Now look, I'm teaching already people the way to correct themselves.
Why are you keeping me?
I already corrected.
I'm teaching people.
And after this, they let me free.
So--
And two months later-- then when they let me free, there's nowhere to go, no--
Pardon me?
I was learning a little bit of my own amateur to paint it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then I left to Kavkaz.
Kavkaz is over there, touristic places.
I used to make pictures in paper, in alabaster.
And I used to sell them in the bazaar.
And I-- the police used to run after me.
I didn't have no license.
They're not giving a license over there.
And I used to, nighttime, to make different kind of pictures.
In Canada, these pictures, they wouldn't even give a penny.
Over there, everything is good.
Yeah.
I used to make-- they used to hang them up on the wall, different kind-- swans and different kind tigers and different-- and I used to sell them.
And I make a little bit money.
I dress up myself.
I was ashamed to come to places where people know me not dressed.
And then I would dress up myself.
I came over there.
I met people.
I came to Rowne.
And I met some friends.
And they told me, Leon, it's time to be married already.
Why, you see, to perform, I didn't go anymore after this.
My morale, morale was low.
And I figure up maybe they'll not let me in the big cities.
And I have a stamp.
I'm stamped already.
As a bad label, you know.
When you're in jail in Russia, bad label in society.
And they're not accepting you.
And I met a cousin, my wife's.
And he told me, I want you to be in my family.
And he give me her address.
And I came.
And she was living with his sister.
And just they came from Siberia, and I saw it.
My-- I, I didn't overprice myself.
I didn't overestimate myself.
I know my nerves is not the same like used to be.
And I see this is the right person to understand me.
And I decided to marry my wife.
We fell in love and--
--sent letters.
And she sent me a couple of letters.
And we married.
And then--
Pardon me?
Oh yeah, excuse me, I missed something.
When I-- before this, when I left, I came to Rowne-- after the jail.
And I decided I want to go to, to Israel.
Then I came to committee to give my papers--
--to apply to let me to go to Israel.
I find out where the-- my mother and my sisters and my brothers is in Israel.
Why I find out-- some people received letters from Israel.
And they received letters-- a Mrs.
Dimerman with the kids is in Israel.
And this is the way through somebody I find out.
And then they said, no, this is only for Polish people and for Jewish.
And you're French.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then I'd gone back to my city, to Kostopol.
I took witnesses, which I have-- this is what I have a paper right now.
And they asked me to publish in newspaper I'm changing my name.
And I published in paper I'm changing my name from this to this.
It was a court with witnesses.
I tell them the reason was admission already.
They have admission for the whole criminal from the army.
They let them free.
They couldn't do me nothing.
I tell them what happened.
Yes.
And they give me my real name.
Identity-- after I left the army.
Yeah.
When I met my wife, I already introduced myself like [LAUGHS] Jewish and Dimerman.
No, it was French.
Yeah.
Yeah, yeah.
Went straight from the army-- after the army, a short time.
After I came out from prison.
Yeah.
A little bit-- make-- you see, very hard for me to remember.
I think-- I think it's after I came out from prison.
Just a moment.
Yeah, the identity, yes, after I came out from prison.
In 1955, yeah.
And I, I met my wife in Vilnius, not in Rowne.
She was in Vilnius.
And I met her over there, which I married my wife.
And they didn't let me-- the police didn't let me to live over there in a big city.
When I married Liuba.
Why, I told you, I have on my passport--
That you were a prisoner.
No-- yes, a special--
I'm not supposed to live so, so far away from a big city.
Then I pay a few underneath the table, and-- 30 kilometers away from the city where Liuba lived.
And they give me a stamp.
I have the right.
Over there, you're supposed to have a stamp in police.
When you came in a new city to live, you have to go in police.
You have to prove that you have a-- excuse me-- a, a place where to live in so many square meters.
And then they give you a permit.
You can live over there.
They give me a permit over there, 30 kilometers away--
--which I have problem, too.
They said you have to stay 105 kilometers away.
Yeah.
Yeah.
[SIGHS]
When I married my wife, I was a foreman.
And, you know, foundations-- they used to make the foundations.
And I learned a little bit the blueprint, slightly.
And I took this job, a foreman.
When they're making the foundations, they're cutting all the foundations, I was this kind of job.
Yeah.
It was very, very low paid, not high wages.
We was living in Troki, a little city, Troki, T-R-O-K.
This is-- was-- used to be-- yeah, Lithuania.
This is from Vilnius, 30 kilometers away.
Well, I think it was around a year.
A year, and then we--
A little bit maybe over a year.
Yes.
And we left.
Son was born--
--my older son.
He was born in 1958.
In case I [LAUGHS] don't make mistake-- very hard time, and I can't remember.
Liuba?
Yeah.
Pardon?
And after that, we make the papers already to go to Poland.
And we left to Poland-- my wife, me, my son.
Yes.
Wroclaw--
Legonice.
Legonice.
Legonice, L-E-G-N-I-C-A.
Legonice.
You see, it's not what I took on Legonice.
I was gone where they sent me.
When we arrived in Poland, there was already a committee which they connected with the ORT organization.
Yes.
And they sent us.
And the UNRRA was over there, too.
And they sent us to Legonice.
In Legonice, they provide us with a place where to live.
Yes-- and a house.
And they give me-- they give me so much money monthly to live and to learn a trade, which I can use for Israel.
Which date-- I have a little bit experience from the army in welding.
And, like, a welder, I was learning over there.
I was in school, and I was learning welding.
And the ORT paid us support.
Yes.
And this give me opportunity to learn to be a welder, which I'm not the only one.
Lots of people was attending the school from the ORT organization.
And then we put application to go to Israel, which I sent my wife first--
--with my son.
She left before me.
'60, yeah.
I was in-- I was over there working, continuing my work, to, to make-- to perfection my--
Yes.
And then I came to Israel.
Yes.
A little bit later, yeah.
I settled in Israel, Kiryat Haim-- Kiryat Haim--
--not far from Haifa.
Yes.
You call this [SPEAKING HEBREW]-- academic people, people with diplomas.
They make-- from Sweden, was donated little [NON-ENGLISH], little houses.
It was nice, comfortable, small little houses.
Comparison-- we came from Russia, we feel comfortable.
And we was living in this housing.
Special was-- this street was maybe two blocks, was little houses.
And this is engineers and lawyers and doctors and teachers.
It was over there, like a-- this, this is the place where they, they give them.
To tell you the truth, in the beginning, when I finished the school, I was finished-- I finished the best, top student.
I was sure they will accept me like an excellent worker.
When I came to Israel and they take me for a test, I couldn't pass the test or show demand and high, high qualification, which I was very disappointed.
I finished with 5-plus.
Yes.
And they told me I am excellent-- not good, just excellent.
And in Israel, they give me a test, and I couldn't pass.
Then they give me the lowest grade.
Then it's OK, I think already for the lowest grade.
Then I realized where I'm going to work is welding boilers, and the boilers under high pressure.
And I was not prepared.
I didn't learn this kind of stuff to do it, just straight weldings.
That's right.
And, and I was welding over there.
I was working in a shop, [NON-ENGLISH], in, in Israel.
And then they laid me off.
When you're over 28, 29, they don't want to-- older people in this time to hold it.
When they're holding you a year, they're obligated to hold you.
They can't fire you.
And-- but to fire you, to lay you off, they have to pay you lots of money.
It's they-- they hold me for 11 months.
And then they let me home.
And then they asked me to come after a week back to work, back from the-- from the-- from the beginning.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I decided not to go to them anymore.
I was disappointed.
And I've gone, like the railway station, [NON-ENGLISH], I've gone over there to work.
Yeah, welding.
Yeah.
And now they did the same thing with me over there, too.
After I didn't even finish the year, they let me go.
They call this [NON-ENGLISH].
They don't want me to have, like, a steady job.
And I was very much disappointed.
I became pessimistic.
I-- when you know you're capable to something else, and you have no opportunity.
And my family in Israel, they're very honest people.
And honest people didn't have it-- money.
They make a nice, balebatish, comfortable living in Israel.
Now, to help me financially, nobody was prepared.
They, they wish they could, as people with a good heart, they could help me.
They didn't have the money.
I was disappointed with myself alone.
You see, I didn't ask for nobody.
I didn't have financial opportunity.
I try in Israel.
I-- my, my-- in my head came different kind of fantasies.

And I tried to open up a new business in Israel-- coffee, coffee, to make coffee different taste, which we have it right now, called different name, different kind of coffee, and coffee to sell direct to the wholesales, groceries, not to have to do it individuals, the manufacturing.
You need money to start it.
And I couldn't find nobody to help me, to go in partnership.
And I see it's a country, which without opportunity, and financial opportunity, you're nothing, especially when you're older.
And they laid me off after 11 months.
I was very disappointed.
I was trying my best to, to work good.
And I figured up they, they will hold me.
And still they didn't hold me.
And this is because the policy in this time was a very tough to get a steady job.
And what happened, happened was my sister was already here.
And we sent her a letter.
And I told my wife, I feel we'll never go to Canada.
Something opportunity will be for us.
We have no chances.
We have no money.
We're poor people.
What we make, we spend for food.
I don't believe to save money for stomach.
And my child's supposed to have what I-- he needed every day.
And I don't drink, I don't smoke, and my wife the same thing.
And we just break even with the money what we, we made.
And this why we decided to leave Israel, why--
--with work is very hard.
And my wife was teaching.
And was-- they considered her a very good teacher.
And your money was-- teachers, at this time, received very little, similar what I received, like, a worker, same money.
I didn't like to leave the country.
I liked very much Israel.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have no choice.
I didn't have a choice.
My sister sponsored me to Canada.
And I came to Canada to prepare a little bit for my-- which I borrowed money to get a ticket.
I didn't have money for a ticket.
I arrived in Canada in 1964.
Pardon me?
I came by myself.
I-- we didn't have money.
I borrowed money for ticket for myself.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I was working hard to--
I was welding.
And I was working.
I received minimum wage.
I didn't know the language.
And these people, where they could understand to speak German, or Polish, Ukrainian, or Jewish, then they used me, and I received minimum wage.
For minimum wage, you can't go too far.
I sent money to my wife, a little bit to pay off my debts, what I borrowed money for a ticket.
And then I prepared a little bit money to make a down payment.
And I was living in Polson, which $50 I paid a month.
My wife came over in-- after a year I was here, in 1963.
And she came over here.
We--
--was-- in the beginning, we was-- one Jewish man, [?
Hoffman, ?] an older man, have a bungalow.
And the wife left him.
He was living alone.
And he told us he will not charge us too much and to live with him.
I was living in Polson, yeah.
With the--
No, no.
I was living in Polson.
I was renting for $60 a month-- $50 a month, one bedroom.
And it was not convenient.
See, one washroom, and the landlady, older lady, and I feel not comfortable to use one washroom with a person what she's not looking so nice left to herself.
She's older age, a nice lady.
And I decided to-- I already make a little bit of money.
And I saved in this time maybe $120 or $130.
And I told my wife-- OK, we paid out a black-and-white TV, in monthly payments.
And I told my wife, time to move out.
And came opportunity, which the wife left him.
And he lived in Seven Oaks, the guy.
He had a house in Seven Oaks.
And he said, [NON-ENGLISH], I don't need the money.
Just come in, whatever you pay me, what you're paying.
I charge you only $80 a month.
I told my wife, $30 the difference, we're going to live comfortable.
And we left.
We gone to him to live-- which I lived in Seven Oaks for two years.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I feel-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you see, with a older man-- my wife was washing up the floor, dirtied the floor, is coming in with his shoes not so clean.
And I don't want my wife to be a housekeeper for him.
And we decided to move to Perth apartments, which I received a raise.
And I quit.
And I start to work as salesman for [?
Steiman. ?]
Yeah, I was a salesman.
And I moved to Perth apartments, in which-- the old [?
Steiman ?] tried to take me without money, nothing to pay.
He told me he'll take me and my wife, the family, to live with him when the wife died.
And I [LAUGHS] have a bad experience to live with the old man.
I told my wife no, no.
We're going to cut down on food.
I don't want it-- to be a housekeeper for somebody.
And we was living in Perth apartments.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] can I continue?
